senses.definition: A navigation device or system that automatically guides the movement of a vehicle, especially an aircraft or spacecraft, without constant human control.
examples: The spacecraft’s autonavigator corrected its course during the long journey.
Modern drones use an onboard autonavigator to follow pre‑programmed routes.

senses.definition: Plural of autonavigator: navigation devices or systems that automatically guide the movement of vehicles without constant human control.
examples: Several autonavigators failed during the test flights.
The lab compared different autonavigators under extreme weather conditions.

senses.definition: The act or process of denying, rejecting, or cancelling oneself, one’s own identity, or one’s own statements.
examples: The philosopher described freedom as a kind of autonegation of pure instinct.
In therapy, patterns of autonegation can appear as constant self‑sabotage.

senses.definition: The surgical removal of one of a patient’s own kidneys.
examples: The patient underwent autonephrectomy after severe kidney damage.
Autonephrectomy may be considered when the affected kidney no longer functions.

senses.definition: A poisonous substance produced within the body that damages the kidneys.
examples: Researchers studied the effects of an autonephrotoxin in animal models.
The buildup of an autonephrotoxin can lead to acute kidney failure.

senses.definition: The science or technology of automatic control and navigation, especially in vehicles and weapons systems.
examples: He specialized in autonetics for guided missile systems.
Advances in autonetics transformed the design of modern aircraft.

senses.definition: A poisonous substance produced within an organism that damages its own nervous system.
examples: The disease is associated with an autoneurotoxin that destroys nerve cells.
Scientists are trying to identify the autoneurotoxin responsible for the symptoms.

senses.definition: A chemical process in which a material forms its own nitride layer, usually by reacting with nitrogen without an external coating step.
examples: Autonitridation of the metal surface improves its hardness and wear resistance.
The alloy showed slow autonitridation under normal atmospheric conditions.

senses.definition: Relating to a special kind of self‑awareness that lets a person mentally place themselves in past or future situations.
examples: Autonoetic consciousness allows us to relive past experiences in vivid detail.
The study explored how autonoetic memory develops in children.

senses.definition: A rare term in rhetoric related to the special use of a person’s own name, sometimes treated as a type of using a proper name to show a quality.
examples: The term autonomasy appears only in specialized discussions of rhetoric.
Some scholars list autonomasy as a sub‑type of name‑based figures of speech.

senses.definition: Relating to the part of the nervous system that controls automatic processes in the body, such as heart rate and digestion.
examples: Stress can disturb the balance of the autonomic nervous system.
The drug affects autonomic functions like sweating and breathing.

senses.definition: Working or happening by itself, without direct human control, especially in technical systems.
examples: Researchers are developing autonomic computing systems that can repair themselves.
The network includes autonomic features to adjust to traffic changes automatically.

senses.definition: Relating to autonomy or to autonomous functions; used mainly in technical or academic writing.
examples: The study focused on autonomical regulation in plant cells.
Some authors avoid the form autonomical and prefer autonomous instead.

senses.definition: In a way that is controlled automatically by the body or by a system, without conscious or direct control.
examples: The heart beats autonomically, without any effort from us.
The server cluster responds autonomically to changes in demand.

senses.definition: The study or use of systems that can manage themselves, especially in computing and engineering.
examples: His research group is a leader in autonomics and self‑managing networks.
Autonomics promises to reduce the cost of large‑scale data centers.

senses.definition: Plural of autonomy: different forms or cases of the right or ability of groups or individuals to control their own lives or affairs.
examples: The agreement granted various autonomies to the country’s regions.
Modern workplaces must balance individual autonomies with team goals.

senses.definition: A person who supports political autonomy for a region or group.
examples: Autonomist leaders called for more powers for the local government.
The party joined forces with autonomists from neighboring provinces.

senses.definition: Plural of autonomist: people who support political autonomy for a region or group.
examples: Autonomists organized mass protests in the capital.
Talks between autonomists and the central government finally resumed.

senses.definition: To make something autonomous; to give it the power to govern or control itself.
examples: The reforms aim to autonomize local schools and hospitals.
Some propose to autonomize the agency so it can act without political pressure.

senses.definition: Having the power or right to govern itself or to make its own decisions independently.
examples: The region became an autonomous republic within the federation.
Universities are seeking more autonomous status from the government.

senses.definition: Able to work or operate by itself, without being directly controlled by people.
examples: Engineers are testing autonomous vehicles on public roads.
The robot can carry out autonomous exploration of the cave.

senses.definition: In an independent way, with the power to make decisions or govern oneself.
examples: The committee can act autonomously from the main board.
Local branches should be able to operate autonomously within clear rules.

senses.definition: In a way that runs by itself, without direct human control, especially in machines and systems.
examples: The drone flies autonomously once a route is programmed.
These software agents negotiate autonomously on behalf of users.

senses.definition: The quality or state of being autonomous.
examples: The autonomousness of the central bank is crucial for economic stability.
They emphasized the autonomousness of the artistic process.

senses.definition: The right or power of a country, region, or group to govern itself.
examples: The province has enjoyed a high level of autonomy since the peace agreement.
The rebels demanded political autonomy from the central government.

senses.definition: The ability to make your own decisions and control your own life.
examples: Teachers are encouraged to give students more autonomy in their learning.
Job autonomy is strongly linked to employee satisfaction.

senses.definition: An official or native name for a group or place, used by the people themselves.
examples: Deutschland is the autonym for what English speakers call Germany.
Many indigenous groups prefer their autonym rather than an external label.

senses.definition: Plural of autonym: official or native names that groups or places use for themselves.
examples: The map lists both exonyms and autonyms for major cities.
Researchers collected the autonyms of all the communities in the region.

senses.definition: A slow reaction in which a substance reacts with oxygen from the air by itself, often causing spoilage or color change.
examples: Autooxidation of fats can make stored food taste rancid.
The paint contains additives that slow autooxidation of the pigments.

senses.definition: A type of parasitism in which an organism or part of an organism acts as a parasite on another part of the same individual or species.
examples: The fungus shows autoparasitism by attacking its own older spores.
Autoparasitism can complicate the life cycle of certain insects.

senses.definition: Relating to a disease or condition that originates within the affected individual rather than from external causes.
examples: The researchers distinguished autopathic disorders from those caused by infection.
An autopathic process develops from the body’s own abnormal responses.

senses.definition: A written account of a person’s own illness or medical experiences.
examples: Her book is an autopathography describing life with chronic pain.
Autopathography can help patients make sense of their suffering.

senses.definition: A disease or disorder that arises from the patient’s own body or mind rather than from external causes.
examples: Some forms of depression are considered a kind of autopathy.
Researchers debated whether the condition should be classed as an autopathy or an infection.

senses.definition: Living in the open sea by one’s own movement, not carried there by currents or attached to something.
examples: Autopelagic species actively swim in the upper layers of the ocean.
The organism changes from a fixed larval stage to an autopelagic adult stage.

senses.definition: A machine that can automatically reproduce a person’s handwritten signature.
examples: The celebrity uses an autopen to sign thousands of photos for fans.
Some officials rely on an autopen for routine correspondence.

senses.definition: Plural of autopen: machines that automatically reproduce a person’s handwritten signature.
examples: The office has several autopens to handle mass mailings.
Autopens have raised questions about the authenticity of signatures.

senses.definition: Normal digestion occurring within the body, especially as an internal self‑digesting process.
examples: Some older texts refer to autopepsia when discussing gastric function.
The term autopepsia is rarely used in modern medical writing.

senses.definition: Plural of autophagous organism; organisms that eat parts of their own bodies.
examples: Certain autophagi consume their own tissues under stress.
The researcher described several autophagi in extreme environments.

senses.definition: The act or condition of eating parts of one’s own body, seen in some animals and rare human disorders.
examples: Autophagia has been reported in rodents during severe starvation.
The psychiatrist treated a patient with episodes of autophagia.

senses.definition: Plural of autophagia: instances or forms of eating parts of one’s own body.
examples: Different autophagias have been documented in captive animals.
The article compares various autophagias in clinical case studies.

senses.definition: Plural of autophagy: processes or acts where cells or organisms break down their own components.
examples: Different autophagies are activated in response to nutrient stress.
The researcher described the various autophagies observed in yeast.

senses.definition: Describing an organism or cell that eats or breaks down parts of its own body.
examples: Autophagous larvae consume their own tissues before pupation.
Certain autophagous cells recycle damaged components to survive.

senses.definition: A natural process in which cells break down and recycle their own damaged parts.
examples: Autophagy helps cells survive during periods of starvation.
Impaired autophagy is linked to several neurodegenerative diseases.

senses.definition: Appearing by itself or seeming to show itself without an obvious external cause; used rarely and mainly in academic or literary contexts.
examples: The vision was described as autophanous, arising without ritual or drugs.
The philosopher spoke of an autophanous truth that reveals itself to the mind.

senses.definition: A strong and unreasonable fear of being alone or of oneself.
examples: She was diagnosed with autophobia after years of panic when left alone.
Autophobia can lead people to stay in unhealthy relationships.

senses.definition: Plural of autophobia: strong and unreasonable fears of being alone or of oneself.
examples: The therapist specialized in treating autophobias and social anxieties.
Different autophobias may appear in childhood and adulthood.

senses.definition: An alternative plural form of autophobia: strong and unreasonable fears of being alone or of oneself.
examples: Older case reports sometimes mention autophobies among neurotic disorders.
Clinicians rarely use the term autophobies today.

senses.definition: Another form of the word autophobia, meaning a strong and unreasonable fear of being alone or of oneself.
examples: The patient’s autophoby made it difficult for her to live independently.
Autophoby is mentioned only in a few older psychological texts.

senses.definition: A device or system that produces sound by itself, often used historically for self‑sounding instruments or signaling devices.
examples: The museum displays an autophon used in early telegraph systems.
An experimental autophon generated tones without a human player.

senses.definition: A self‑acting sound‑producing device or instrument, especially in older technical usage.
examples: The autophone was one of several inventions for automatic musical performance.
Engineers demonstrated an autophone that could transmit coded signals.

senses.definition: Plural of autophony: abnormal experiences of hearing one’s own voice or internal sounds unusually loudly.
examples: Patients described various autophonies, including loud breathing and chewing.
Eustachian tube disorders often cause disturbing autophonies.

senses.definition: A device designed to show or measure the vibrations of sounds produced by the voice itself.
examples: The researcher used an autophonoscope to study vowel production.
Early speech laboratories relied on crude autophonoscopes.

senses.definition: Producing sound by itself, without an external player; self‑sounding.
examples: An autophonous device emitted a tone whenever pressure changed.
The installation includes several autophonous sculptures.

senses.definition: An abnormal condition in which a person hears their own voice or internal sounds, such as breathing or chewing, unusually loudly.
examples: Autophony is a common symptom of Eustachian tube problems.
The patient complained of autophony and echoing when speaking.
